text: Sociologist Sherry Turkle coined the phrase 'always on, always on you' to describe the smartphone's role in collapsing the boundary between presence and absence. Before mobile devices, missing a conversation meant being genuinely absent — spatially elsewhere. FOMO, fear of missing out, is the psychological residue of a world that now makes every absence feel chosen and therefore culpable. You are not just away; you chose not to check. This shift from structural to moral absence is what makes FOMO so corrosive. The guilt is not irrational — the technology has genuinely changed what absence means.
question: According to the passage, what has changed about the nature of absence in the smartphone era?

options.text: Absence has become less frequent because people are more connected.
Absence now feels chosen and morally culpable rather than merely structural.
Absence triggers anxiety because people fear being forgotten by their social networks.
Sherry Turkle argued that absence was always a source of guilt, even before mobile devices.
